cases.expected: Uses the validate, search, dry-run, inspect, and apply ladder with an explicit TypeScript pattern.
cases.actual: This is a syntax-tree question, so I use ast-grep. First I validate the pattern with `ast-grep run --lang ts --pattern 'client.fetch($URL)' --debug-query`, then search without mutation (`... src/`), then dry-run `--rewrite 'http.fetch($URL)'` without `--update-all` to preview. I verify by inspecting the blast radius — total matches, changed files, and edge forms like optional chaining — and only then re-run with `--update-all`, checking the diff and focused tests.

cases.expected: Routes to text search because this is a bytes question, not a syntax-tree question.
cases.actual: "breaking change" is a bytes/prose target, not a code structure, so ast-grep is the wrong tool. I use plain text search like `grep -rn 'breaking change'` (or ripgrep) over the release-notes files. I verify by reading the matched lines in context to confirm they are the intended exact-phrase occurrences.

cases.expected: Routes to semantic LSP tooling because symbol identity and references are required.
cases.actual: Renaming a symbol and updating references is a semantic, type-aware task, so I use LSP/semantic tooling (e.g. lsp_rename after lsp_prepare_rename) rather than ast-grep, which would match syntactic text and miss scope/type ownership. I first run lsp_find_references on UserService to see the true reference set, then apply the rename. I verify by re-checking references resolve, the diff touches only genuine bindings, and typecheck/build passes; ast-grep would only help to catch syntactic variants outside the one resolved symbol.
